questions.question: What is the primary difference between Authentication and Authorization?
options: They are synonymous and can be used interchangeably in server design
Authentication verifies WHO the user is (Login); Authorization verifies WHAT they are allowed to do (Permissions)
Authentication checks permissions; Authorization validates the password hash
Authentication uses JWTs; Authorization uses session cookies
questions.answer: Authentication verifies WHO the user is (Login); Authorization verifies WHAT they are allowed to do (Permissions)
questions.explanation: You authenticate a user by verifying their password. Once authenticated, you authorize them when they attempt to delete a post to ensure they have the 'Admin' role.

questions.question: Why must you NEVER store user passwords in plain text in your database?
options: Plain text takes up too much disk space
If a data breach occurs, hackers instantly have the passwords to access user accounts across the internet (due to password reuse)
SQL databases cannot process text efficiently in WHERE clauses
It causes performance issues during the login lookup
questions.answer: If a data breach occurs, hackers instantly have the passwords to access user accounts across the internet (due to password reuse)
questions.explanation: Passwords must always be hashed (one-way encryption) using a slow algorithm like Bcrypt with a salt, so that even if the database is leaked, the original passwords remain secure.

questions.question: Which statement about the data payload of a JSON Web Token (JWT) is true?
options: It is heavily encrypted and unreadable to anyone without the server secret
It is only Base64 encoded, meaning anyone intercepting it can easily decode and read the data inside it
It automatically expires 24 hours after being issued regardless of configuration
It can safely store a user's unhashed password
questions.answer: It is only Base64 encoded, meaning anyone intercepting it can easily decode and read the data inside it
questions.explanation: The JWT signature prevents the payload from being tampered with, but it does NOT encrypt the payload. You must never place sensitive information (banking info, SSNs) inside the JWT token.

questions.question: How do stateless JWTs differ from traditional Server-Side Session architecture?
options: They require the database to lookup the user's session ID on every request
Because the JWT contains everything needed to verify the user mathematically (via the signature), the server doesn't need to look up a session in memory or the database
They can only be used on HTTPS connections, whereas sessions run on HTTP
JWTs are much larger in size than session IDs and slow down the network immensely
questions.answer: Because the JWT contains everything needed to verify the user mathematically (via the signature), the server doesn't need to look up a session in memory or the database
questions.explanation: Server sessions are 'stateful', meaning the server must store the session. JWTs are 'stateless'. The server verifies the crypto-signature is intact, and trusts the decoded payload immediately.

questions.question: What security problem does the Refresh Token architecture solve?
options: It prevents Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
It solves the inability to easily invalidate/logout truly stateless Access Tokens by making the Access Token expire in a few minutes
It encrypts the JSON payload automatically
It stops SQL injection on the login page
questions.answer: It solves the inability to easily invalidate/logout truly stateless Access Tokens by making the Access Token expire in a few minutes
questions.explanation: If an attacker steals a 30-day JWT, they have access for 30 days. By making the Access JWT last 15 minutes, but giving the user a stateful 30-day Refresh Token, you can easily revoke theRefresh token if a 'logout' is requested.
